fields.brief_block: Moreover, the Supreme Court confirmed in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, 554–55 (1965), that even where traffic regulations are validly implicated, the government cannot use them as a pretext to suppress protected political expression.
fields.finding_analysis: Cox reverses appellant's obstructing-public-passages conviction because Baton Rouge applied its street-obstruction statute through standardless, unfettered official discretion that functioned as the equivalent of a discretionary licensing scheme — a 'device for the suppression of the communication of ideas' — even while the Court acknowledged the State's legitimate power to regulate streets for traffic and safety. The opinion squarely supports the proposition, holding the practice 'an unwarranted abridgment of appellant's freedom of speech and assembly secured to him by the First Amendment.' The 554–55 pinpoint correctly frames the street-regulation-versus-free-speech issue the Court resolved against the State.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court established in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 571, 571–73 (1965), that police must give notice of a dispersal order and must not begin arresting demonstrators until that notice has been communicated.
fields.finding_analysis: The brief cites Cox v. Louisiana at 379 U.S. 571, which is the separate companion opinion (No. 49, the courthouse-picketing case), but the opinion provided here is the distinct No. 24 majority reported at 379 U.S. 536, whose star-pagination ends at page 558 and which closes by directing the reader to the later opinions beginning at page 575 and 591. Page 571 and the cited 571–73 range are not contained in this document, so the provided text cannot ground the dispersal-order-notice proposition. For what it is worth, the 536 opinion reverses on free-speech and vagueness grounds and announces no rule requiring police to communicate a dispersal order before arresting; the legal effect of the order is among the matters this opinion defers to No. 49.

fields.brief_block: [T]he government cannot entrap citizens "by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." Id. at 746 (quoting Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. at 571).
fields.finding_analysis: The brief cites Cox v. Louisiana at 379 U.S. 571 for the privilege/entrapment principle, but the file provided is the No. 24 opinion at 379 U.S. 536, which ends at page 558 and does not include page 571 — that pinpoint belongs to the companion No. 49 opinion. The provided 536 opinion's footnote 3 confirms the mismatch, stating that the 'permission' granted to the students 'is discussed at greater length in No. 49, where its legal effect is considered'; the holding the brief invokes thus lives in the companion opinion not contained in this file, so the proposition cannot be verified from the text supplied. The quoted language was also independently flagged FABRICATED.

fields.brief_block: Under Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, and Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied the order.
fields.finding_analysis: Cox v. Louisiana (379 U.S. 536) is a First Amendment case reversing a single demonstration leader's convictions for disturbing the peace (on free-speech and vagueness grounds) and for obstructing public passages (on standardless-discretion grounds). It does not address mass-arrest standards, individualized probable cause that each arrestee heard and willfully defied a dispersal order, or any requirement that a dispersal order be communicated person-by-person. Although the facts recite a sheriff's order to break up the demonstration, the decision turns on the constitutionality of the statutes, not on per-arrestee probable cause; that doctrine derives from the modern mass-arrest line the brief pairs it with, not from this opinion.
